claim: Rocq/Coq is an interactive proof assistant for formal specifications and proofs, with extraction to executable programs.
verified_with: CompCert and Rocq official pages describe Coq/Rocq-backed verification/extraction.

claim: Lean is an open-source programming language and proof assistant for formally verified code.
verified_with: Mathlib page confirms broad formalized math library ecosystem.

claim: seL4 has machine-checked mathematical proofs for Arm, RISC-V, and Intel architectures.
verified_with: ACM seL4 formal verification paper independently documents the verified OS-kernel work.

claim: CompCert is a formally verified C compiler with machine-checked proof that generated code follows source semantics.
verified_with: CompCert GitHub overview repeats the verified compiler claim.

claim: Dafny is a verification-aware language with native specifications and a static verifier.
verified_with: Dafny GitHub describes verifier feedback and compilation targets.

claim: Frama-C is an open-source platform for C source-code analysis fueled by formal methods.
verified_with: Frama-C publications describe industrial-size C verification work.

claim: Z3 is an SMT solver for symbolic logic and a foundation for software engineering verification tools.
verified_with: Microsoft Z3 Guide describes satisfiability checking over multiple theories.

claim: TLA+ is useful for specifying concurrent and distributed systems, with TLAPS mechanically checking proofs.
verified_with: Lamport ACM paper describes TLA+ for specifying/verifying systems.

claim: Hypothesis is Python property-based testing: tests should pass for all inputs in a described range and the tool generates cases.
verified_with: HypothesisWorks site gives the same description.

claim: Coverage-guided fuzzing uses instrumentation to guide input mutation toward new coverage.
verified_with: AFL docs describe compile-time instrumentation and generated interesting test cases.

claim: SWE-bench Verified is a human-filtered subset of 500 instances for evaluating AI software issue resolution.
verified_with: OpenAI announcement describes human-validated SWE-bench subset.

claim: Wolfram Language is a broad symbolic computational language with high-level domain objects and built-in knowledge.
verified_with: Wolfram Mathematica page describes thousands of functions and real-world data.

claim: Scratch, Blockly, and Node-RED represent accessible block/flow systems for working programs.
verified_with: Blockly docs and Node-RED GitHub confirm visual/block/low-code flow programming.

claim: SCBE Machine Crystal currently produces executable/rejected receipts across shape programs, p/n/e conservation, Bhargava overlays, and geometry relation maps.
